results.source_used: Found in BOTH candidate volumes. PROPOSING E-1 volume (/tmp/sl1893.txt, lines 14131-14145) under heading "PROHIBITING LOTTERIES" / "Proposed Amendments to the Constitution" — concurrent resolution referring the amendment to the next general election per Article 15, with a Secretary of State (C. M. Dahl) certificate that it "will be submitted to a vote of the people at the next general election." APPROVED E+1 volume (/tmp/sl1895.txt, lines 2961-2966) prints the ratified text appended to the constitution under "AMENDMENT TO CONSTITUTION." I rely primarily on the 1895 (E+1) approved volume for the enacted in-force wording, cross-checked against the 1893 (E-1) proposing resolution's "AMENDMENT." block — both independently confirm the same operative text.

findings.note: PUNCTUATION DEVIATION IN STORED TEXT. Stored reads "...for any purpose, and shall pass laws..." with a comma after "purpose." BOTH session-law renderings have NO comma there. 1893 proposing block: "...for any purpose ancl shall pass laws..." ("ancl"=OCR for "and"). 1895 enacted appended text: "...for any purpose and shall pass laws to prohibit the sale of lottery or gift enterprise tickets." Two independent renderings agree the comma is absent, so this is not OCR loss — the defect is in the STORED text (an extra comma not in the enacted session law). All wording otherwise matches verbatim. Capitalization "Legislative Assembly" in the session law vs. stored "legislative assembly" is a normalization choice, not flagged as substantive.
findings.sessionlaw_text: The Legislative Assembly shall have no power to authorize lotteries or gift enterprises for any purpose and shall pass laws to prohibit the sale of lottery or gift enterprise tickets.

results.metadata_issues: Stored authority is a placeholder: "Amendment ratified Nov. 6, 1894" with election_date 1894-11-06 and effective_date 1894-12-06. The session-law excerpts do NOT contain a vote canvass or proclamation for this amendment (the canvass/proclamation language present in /tmp/sl1895.txt is from the 1889 Enabling Act, not this amendment). The 1893 proposing volume supplies the proposing authority: a House/Senate concurrent resolution of the Third Legislative Assembly, certified by Secretary of State C. M. Dahl, referred to the next general election under Article 15 of the Constitution. No vote tally is present in either excerpt. Recommend backfilling authority to reference the Third Legislative Assembly concurrent resolution (Session Laws 1893, "Proposed Amendments to the Constitution — Prohibiting Lotteries") plus ratification at the 1894 general election; the precise ratification/canvass date is not established by these scans. Target designation "amend. art. I" and the constitutional amending authority (Art. 15, the 1889 procedure) are consistent — no metadata defect in article/section numbering.

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume (E-1): /tmp/sl1897.txt, "PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO THE CONSTITUTION" / "CONCURRENT RESOLUTION" at p. 349 (lines ~20722-20748). This volume holds the resolution proposing to amend §§121 and 127, submitted to the qualified electors at the next general election (1898). The 1889 original-Constitution front matter (lines ~1432, 1471) confirms the amendment's substance: original §121 had THREE elector classes (incl. foreign-birth declarants) and original §127 lacked any educational test, whereas the proposed amendment reduces §121 to two classes and adds the educational-test clause to §127 — matching the stored text. The E+1 1899 volume's only §121/§127 hit (line 7718) is an unrelated bill-of-rights annotation, not this amendment.

findings.note: Stored text matches the proposing concurrent resolution verbatim. SL scan reads 'belong-/ing', 'qualified elector', two classes (First. Citizens of the United States; Second. Civilized persons of Indian descent ... severed their tribal relations two years next preceding such election). All identical to stored; no divergent words. Defect (if any) is only minor line-break hyphenation in the SCAN. Stored text is clean and correct.
findings.sessionlaw_text:
Every male person of the age of 21 years or upwards, belonging to either of the following classes who shall have resided in the state one year and in the county six months and in the precinct ninety days next preceding any election shall be a qualified elector at such election.

First. Citizens of the United States.

Second. Civilized persons of Indian descent, who shall have severed their tribal relations two years next preceding such election.

findings.note: Stored text matches the proposing resolution. The only divergences are OCR garble in the SCAN, not the STORED text: SL scan reads 'non campus 11Untis' (= 'non compos mentis'), 'renalties' (= 'penalties'), and 'genera election' (= 'general election'). The stored record correctly has 'non compos mentis', 'penalties', and 'general election'. Stored text is clean and correct; defect is in the session-law scan only.
findings.sessionlaw_text: No person who is under guardianship, non compos mentis, or insane, shall be qualified to vote at any election, nor shall any person convicted of treason or felony, unless restored to civil rights, and the legislature shall by law establish an educational test as a qualification, and may prescribe penalties for failing, neglecting or refusing to vote at any general election.

results.metadata_issues: Authority is a placeholder ('Amendment ratified 1898-11-08'). The authoritative session-law source is the 1897 Concurrent Resolution proposing the amendment (Session Laws of North Dakota 1897, "Proposed Amendments to the Constitution," p. 349), submitted to the qualified electors at the 1898 general election. NOTE: This proposing (E-1) volume contains NO Governor's-approval line for the §121/§127 resolution (concurrent resolutions submitting proposed amendments were not signed; the nearby 'Approved March 10, 1897' line belongs to the separate §158 resolution that follows). It also contains NO vote canvass/tally — that would appear only in the post-election record. Stored election_date 1898-11-08 and effective_date 1898-12-08 cannot be confirmed or refuted from these scans; consider backfilling authority to cite '1897 N.D. Sess. Laws, Proposed Constitutional Amendments, p. 349 (concurrent resolution); ratified at the general election of Nov. 1898.'

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume (E-1): /tmp/sl1899.txt, "PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO CONSTITUTION — CONCURRENT RESOLUTION [To be submitted at the General Election of 1900]", "§ I. Section 76 of article 3 of the constitution," text at lines 11430-11459. The 1901 (E+1) volume (/tmp/sl1901.txt) does NOT carry the constitutional amendment: its "Section 76" (line 8204) is a statutory Revised-Codes section (printing/binding), and its "Board of Pardons" act (Chapter, S.B. 6, line 1809) is implementing legislation, not the constitutional text. The prior pass's wrong-volume error was almost certainly mistaking that 1901 statutory board-of-pardons act for the amendment. The convention for this 1900-ratified amendment placed the operative language in the E-1 proposing volume.
results.metadata_issues: Stored authority is a placeholder: authority="Amendment ratified 1900-11-06". The session-law source is a concurrent resolution of the Fifth (5th) legislative assembly published in the 1899 Session Laws under "PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO CONSTITUTION — CONCURRENT RESOLUTION [To be submitted at the General Election of 1900]," submitting the § 76 / art. 3 amendment to the people at the next general election. Authority could be backfilled to cite that 1899 concurrent resolution (5th Legislative Assembly, Proposed Amendments to Constitution). No "Approved <date>" line and NO vote canvass/tally appears for this amendment in either volume: the 1899 volume only proposes it; the 1901 volume carries only the implementing Board of Pardons statute, not a ratification canvass. So the stored election_date 1900-11-06 and effective_date 1900-12-06 cannot be corroborated by a tally in these two files; they are consistent with the "submitted at the General Election of 1900" recital but otherwise unverified here.

findings.note: Single load-bearing divergence: the noun number of the pardon body. The enacted session-law text reads "board of pardon" (SINGULAR) in BOTH places it appears: (1) "in conjunction with the board of pardon of which the governor shall be ex-officio a member" (sl1899.txt line 11433) and (2) "pardon granted by the board of pardon, stating the name of the convict" (line 11456). The STORED text reads "board of pardons" (PLURAL) in both spots. The defect is in the STORED text (it normalized to plural); the session-law scan reads singular cleanly. Caveat (why medium, not high): the divergence is a one-letter 's' difference and the OCR around these lines is otherwise noisy ("§ 7f)"=§76, "tl.e"=the, "oi"=of, "Stat~"=State, "app0inted"=appointed, "remission or fine"=remission of fine, "shalt"=shall). However the words "board of pardon" themselves render cleanly and CONSISTENTLY singular twice, so this is more likely the genuine enacted wording than a doubled OCR drop. Countervailing context: the contemporaneous 1901 implementing statute (sl1901.txt line 1809 ff., "PROCEEDINGS OF BOARD OF PARDONS") uses "board of pardons" (plural) throughout, so plural was the drafters' usual usage. Recommendation: treat as a likely genuine source quirk and preserve the singular per the preserve-source-typos policy, OR at minimum flag the stored plural as unverified against the session law. Minor non-substantive diffs (all OCR/style, stored is fine): stored "ex officio" vs SL "ex-officio" (hyphenation); SL "offonses"/"shalt"/"remission or fine" are OCR noise matching the stored "offenses"/"shall"/"remission of fine." The three-paragraph structure, the treason-suspension paragraph, and the communicate-to-legislative-assembly paragraph all otherwise match the stored text verbatim. The stored heading "Board of pardons" is an editorial caption (no such caption in the constitutional resolution text) and is not itself a defect.
findings.sessionlaw_text:
Section 76 of article 3 of the constitution of the State of North Dakota is amended so as to read as follows:

§ 76. The governor shall have power in conjunction with the board of pardon of which the governor shall be ex-officio a member and the other members of which shall consist of the attorney general of the State of North Dakota, the chief justice of the Supreme Court of the State of North Dakota and two qualified electors who shall be appointed by the governor, to remit fines and forfeitures, to grant reprieves, commutations and pardons after conviction for all offenses except treason and cases of impeachment; but the legislative assembly may by law regulate the manner in which the remission of fines, pardons, commutations and reprieves may be applied for.

Upon conviction for treason the governor shall have the power to suspend the execution of sentence until the case shall be reported to the legislative assembly at its next regular session, when the legislative assembly shall either pardon or commute the sentence, direct the execution of the sentence or grant further reprieve.

The governor shall communicate to the legislative assembly at each regular session each case of remission of fine, reprieve, commutation or pardon granted by the board of pardon, stating the name of the convict, the crime for which he is convicted, the sentence and its date and the date of the remission, commutation, pardon or reprieve, with their reasons for granting the same.

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume E-1: /tmp/sl1899.txt, "CONCURRENT RESOLUTION ... PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO CONSTITUTION" (p. 261), lines ~11537-11578. This is the correct source: it is the proposed amendment of section 179, "agreed to by the Fifth legislative assembly ... and the same is hereby agreed to, and said amendment shall be submitted to the people at the next general election" (the 1900 election). The E+1 volume sl1901's constitutional-amendment section (line ~12607ff) proposes amendments to DIFFERENT sections (176, 215, 158) for a later (eighth-assembly) cycle and contains NO §179 amendment and NO §179 vote canvass — confirming this 1900-ratified §179 amendment lives only in the E-1 proposing volume.

findings.note: Stored text matches the session law verbatim: after normalizing case/punctuation, both are exactly 237 words and identical. The only divergences are OCR artifacts in the SCAN, not in the stored text: the scan renders several commas as periods ('property.', 'situated.', 'township.', 'companies.') and drops one comma ('villages townships' for stored 'villages, townships'). Stored text is clean and correct; no substantive or wording defect.
findings.sessionlaw_text: All property, except as hereinafter in this section provided, shall be assessed in the county, city, township, town, village or district in which it is situated, in the manner prescribed by law. The franchise, roadway, roadbed, rails and rolling stock of all railroads and the franchise and all other property of all express companies, freight line companies, car equipment companies, sleeping car companies, dining car companies, telegraph or telephone companies or corporations operated in this state and used directly or indirectly in the carrying of persons, property or messages, shall be assessed by the state board of equalization at their actual value and such assessed value shall be apportioned to the counties, cities, towns, villages, townships and districts in which such railroad companies, express companies, sleeping car companies, dining car companies, telegraph and telephone companies are located or through which they are operated as a basis for the taxation of such property, in proportion to the number of miles of such property within such counties, cities, towns, villages, townships and districts or over which any part of such property is used or operated within such counties, towns, villages, townships and districts. But should any railroad allow any portion of its roadway to be used for any purpose other than the operation of a railroad thereon, such portion of its roadway, while so used, shall be assessed in the manner provided for the assessment of other real property.

results.metadata_issues: Heading: stored caption "Assessment of railroads, express, telegraph, etc. by state board of equalization" is consistent with the session-law description "relating to the assessment of railroads, express companies, freight line companies, car equipment companies, sleeping car companies, dining car companies, telegraph companies and telephone companies." No metadata defect in the stored text. Authority backfill opportunity (not an error, just thin provenance): stored authority is the placeholder "Amendment ratified 1900-11-06". The session law shows the real legislative provenance — the §179 amendment was "agreed to by the Fifth legislative assembly," re-agreed by the Sixth legislative assembly (1899 session, /tmp/sl1899.txt p. 261), and "submitted to the people at the next general election" (the Nov. 1900 general election). No approval date or vote tally appears in EITHER volume for this amendment: the proposing E-1 volume carries only the resolution language, and the E+1 (1901) volume contains no canvass for §179. Stored election_date 1900-11-06 and effective_date 1900-12-06 are consistent with ratification at the 1900 general election; neither could be cross-checked against a tally because no canvass exists in these scans.

results.source_used: /tmp/sl1903.txt — the E-1 PROPOSING volume (1903 Session Laws), under the "PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO CONSTITUTION" / "CONCURRENT RESOLUTION" section. Two resolutions amend N.D. Const. § 215: (a) Subdivision 5 [H.B. No. 73, Davis, "Name of School for Deaf and Dumb," Approved March 4, 1903] at lines ~13340-13396, and (b) Subdivision 8 [S.B. No. 68, Cashel, "Location of Institution for Feeble Minded," Approved March 5, 1903] at lines ~13320-13374. These match THIS amendment: proposed/agreed-to resolutions referred for submission to the qualified electors at the next general election (Nov. 1904), per § 202. The E+1 (1905) volume /tmp/sl1905.txt was checked and contains NO § 215 constitutional amendment or ratification canvass — only statutory appropriation/election-code text — confirming the proposing-volume convention for this era.

findings.target: N.D. Const. § 215 — Subdivision Fifth (school for the deaf and dumb)
findings.note: Stored: 'Fifth. The school for the deaf and dumb of North Dakota, at the city of Devils Lake, in the county of Ramsey.' Session law (H.B. 73, Approved March 4, 1903) reads verbatim the same, word-for-word and case-for-case. No divergence.
findings.sessionlaw_text: Fifth. The school for the deaf and dumb of North Dakota, at the city of Devils Lake, in the county of Ramsey.

findings.target: N.D. Const. § 215 — Subdivision Eighth (insane hospital; feeble-minded institution)
findings.note: All WORDS and NUMBERS are identical; the divergences are capitalization and one punctuation mark, and they are in the STORED text (the 1903 scan here is clean, not garbled). Enacted session law uses lowercase 'city of Jamestown, in the county of Stutsman' and 'city of Grafton, in the county of Walsh' — STORED has title-case 'City'/'County' in all four spots. Enacted reads lowercase quoted phrase WITH a trailing comma inside/after the close-quote: 'for "other educational and charitable institutions," to the benefit' — STORED has title-case '"Other Educational and Charitable Institutions"' and DROPS the comma after the closing quote. These look like a later codified/Blue-Book-style normalization carried into the stored record rather than the as-enacted 1903 text. Meaning is unchanged, but for an authoritative-text DB the enacted lowercase form with the comma is the controlling session-law text.
findings.sessionlaw_text: Eighth. A state hospital for the insane at the city of Jamestown, in the county of Stutsman. And the legislative assembly shall appropriate twenty thousand acres of the grant of lands made by the act of congress aforesaid for "other educational and charitable institutions," to the benefit and for the endowment of said institution, and there shall be located at or near the city of Grafton, in the county of Walsh, an institution for the feeble minded, on the grounds purchased by the secretary of the interior for a penitentiary building.

results.metadata_issues: AUTHORITY: Stored authority is the placeholder-style 'Amendments V & VI, ratified Nov. 8, 1904'. The session law provides better provenance worth backfilling: the two amendments to § 215 originated as concurrent resolutions in the 1903 (8th) Legislative Assembly — Subdivision 8 from S.B. No. 68 (Cashel), 'Changing the Location of the Institution for Feeble Minded from Jamestown to Grafton,' Approved March 5, 1903; and Subdivision 5 from H.B. No. 73 (Davis), 'Relating to Name of School for Deaf and Dumb,' Approved March 4, 1903. Both were 'adopted by the seventh legislative assembly ... referred to the eighth legislative assembly for approval,' agreed to, and 'submitted to the qualified electors ... at the next general election' (the Nov. 1904 election) per § 202. DATES: Stored election_date 1904-11-08 and effective_date 1904-12-08 are consistent with ratification at the Nov. 1904 general election; the 'Approved March 4/5, 1903' lines are the LEGISLATIVE approval of the proposing resolutions, NOT a later (different-year) amendment — do not mistake them for a competing § 215 amendment. VOTE TALLY: No canvass/vote count appears in either volume; the 1905 (E+1) volume contains no ratification canvass for § 215, so no tally can be supplied from these session-law scans.

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume (E-1): /tmp/sl1903.txt, "PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO CONSTITUTION — CONCURRENT RESOLUTION — TAXING GRAIN IN ELEVATORS" [H. B. No. 63-Chaffee], lines ~13258-13294 (amends §176 by adding the grain clause; "Approved March 2, 1903"). The E+1 volume (/tmp/sl1905.txt) was checked and contains NO §176 constitutional amendment or vote canvass — its grain/elevator hits are ordinary statutory chapters (warehouse receipts, grain inspection). The convention for this 1904-ratified amendment was the proposing-resolution form in the 1903 volume.

findings.note: The session-law amendment (1903 proposing resolution, H.B. No. 63-Chaffee, 'TAXING GRAIN IN ELEVATORS') amends §176 by ADDING ONLY the grain clause. That added clause matches the stored text VERBATIM: stored = 'The legislative assembly may further provide that grain grown within the state and held therein in elevators, warehouses and granaries may be taxed at a fixed rate.'; session law (de-hyphenated across the line break) = identical word-for-word. The session-law SCAN has the usual pdftotext line-wrap/hyphenation noise ('gran-\naries', 'further provide that grain grown' wrapped) but no textual divergence. The stored record additionally reproduces the full pre-existing §176 body ('Laws shall be passed taxing by uniform rule all property according to its true value in money...'); that base body is NOT restated in this amendment's text (the 1903 resolution only adds the clause), so it is not testable against THIS amendment — it is the standing section text into which the clause was inserted. No defect in the stored amendment clause. (Note for the record, not a defect attributable to this amendment: the stored base body contains 'religions, cemetery' where the historical §176 reads 'religious'; this lives in the inherited section body, not in the language added by Amendment 7.)
findings.sessionlaw_text: The legislative assembly may further provide that grain grown within the state and held therein in elevators, warehouses and granaries may be taxed at a fixed rate.

results.metadata_issues: Session law shows the proposing concurrent resolution "Approved March 2, 1903" (resolution-approval date, NOT the ratification date). The resolution recites that the amendment was "adopted by the seventh legislative assembly ... and by it referred to the eighth legislative assembly ... for approval or rejection," then "submitted to the qualified electors of the state for approval or rejection, in accordance with the provisions of section 202." No vote canvass/tally is printed in EITHER volume (the 1905 approved volume has no §176 amendment or canvass). Stored metadata: effective_date 1904-12-08, election_date 1904-11-08, authority "Amendment VII, ratified Nov. 8, 1904." The election/ratification dates cannot be confirmed FROM these session-law files (no canvass present); the only session-law date is the March 2, 1903 resolution approval. The placeholder-style authority "Amendment VII, ratified Nov. 8, 1904" could be enriched with the proposing citation: 1903 N.D. Sess. Laws, Proposed Amendments to Constitution, Concurrent Resolution (H.B. No. 63), approved March 2, 1903 (proposing the grain-in-elevators clause to §176).

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume (E-1): /tmp/sl1907.txt, "CONCURRENT RESOLUTION [S. B. No. 334] Amending Section 162 of the Constitution... Relating to the Investment of Permanent School Funds" (lines ~24496-24527). This is the resolution submitting the §162 amendment "to the qualified electors of the state at the next general election" (the 1908 election) per §202. The 1909 (E+1) volume does NOT contain a §162 constitutional canvass — its "INVESTMENT OF PERMANENT SCHOOL FUND" passage (Chapter 106, S.B. 27, lines ~6929-6970) is a STATUTORY amendment to §155 of the Political Code, not the constitutional §162, and the 1909 "PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO CONSTITUTION" block (line ~18363+) proposes §§216/112/91/158 for the 1910 election, not §162. The prior wrong-volume pass likely mismatched the 1909 §155 statute. Correct authoritative source for amendment 8 is the 1907 volume.

findings.note: Stored enacted text matches the 1907 session-law verbatim after dehyphenating OCR line-break splits. The only divergences are SCAN OCR noise, not stored defects: hyphenated word-wraps in the scan ('school cor porations', 'have never re pudiated', 're pudiated any', 'first mortgages on farm' wrapped), inserted whitespace, and a stray period vs comma in the boilerplate ('agreed to.' in scan vs 'agreed to,'). Substantive enacted words all match: 'invested only in bonds of school corporations or of counties, or of townships, or of municipalities within the state', 'provided, such states have never repudiated any of their indebtedness, or on first mortgages on farm lands in this state, not exceeding in amount one-third of the actual value of any subdivision ... determined by the board of appraisal of school lands.' Note: stored editorial heading 'Investment of permanent school and educational funds' differs from the resolution caption 'Relating to the Investment of Permanent School Funds' (caption omits 'and educational'), but the heading is descriptive metadata, not enacted text, so it is not a substantive defect.
findings.sessionlaw_text: § 162. The moneys of the permanent school fund and other educational funds shall be invested only in bonds of school corporations or of counties, or of townships, or of municipalities within the state, bonds issued for the construction of drains under authority of law within the state, bonds of the United States, bonds of the state of North Dakota, bonds of other states; provided, such states have never repudiated any of their indebtedness, or on first mortgages on farm lands in this state, not exceeding in amount one-third of the actual value of any subdivision on which the same may be loaned, such value to be determined by the board of appraisal of school lands.

results.metadata_issues: The proposing (E-1) volume gives no vote tally/canvass — that is expected, because canvass numbers appear only in an E+1 approved volume, and here there is none for §162 (the 1909 §155 item is a statute). Authority found in the session law: 1907 concurrent resolution S.B. No. 334, the amendment having been "adopted by the ninth legislative assembly ... and by it referred to the tenth legislative assembly ... for approval or rejection," "agreed to" and "submitted to the qualified electors of the state at the next general election ... in accordance with the provisions of section 202 of the constitution"; "Filed in the office of the secretary of state March 23, 1907." Stored authority "Amendment ratified 1908-11-03" is a placeholder that could be backfilled with: proposed by 1907 concurrent resolution (S.B. 334) under N.D. Const. § 202, ratified at the general election of Nov. 3, 1908. Stored election_date 1908-11-03 and effective_date 1908-12-03 are consistent with ratification at the 1908 general election; the session law itself does not state these dates (it is the proposing instrument).

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume (E-1), /tmp/sl1907.txt, "CONCURRENT RESOLUTION [S. B. No. 302] — A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION Amending Section 158 of the Constitution of the State of North Dakota, Relating to the Sale of State Lands" (lines 24522-24579, "Filed in the office of the secretary of state March 23, 1907"). This resolution was adopted by the ninth legislative assembly and referred to the tenth, "to be submitted to the qualified electors of the state at the next general election" (the 1908 election) — matching this amendment. The 1909 (E+1) volume's two §158 resolutions are DIFFERENT, LATER amendments (five-per-cent interest, filed March 16/11, 1909, tenth->eleventh assembly, plus a railroad-lands variant) and do not match the stored text.

findings.note: Stored text matches the 1907 proposing resolution (S.B. 302) verbatim; difflib ratio 0.97. Every divergence is in the session-law SCAN, not the stored record: space-before-punctuation pdftotext artifacts (SCAN 'follows :', 'void ;', 'therein ;' vs stored 'follows:', 'void;', 'therein;') and hyphenation line-break splits (SCAN 'circu lation', 'sub divided', 'here tofore', 'pur poses' vs stored 'circulation', 'subdivided', 'heretofore', 'purposes'). Distinctive matching features present in both: heading 'Minimum price of state lands', 'six per centum' interest, 'and one at the seat of government', and the clause 'and no such contract heretofore made shall be held void for non-payment of taxes accruing on the lands described therein; provided, such taxes shall have been paid before this amendment takes effect'. The stored text is clean and correct; no edit needed.
findings.sessionlaw_text: § 158. Minimum price of state lands. No land shall be sold for less than the appraised value and in no case for less than ten dollars per acre. The purchaser shall pay one-fifth of the price in cash, and the remaining four-fifths as follows: One-fifth in five years, one-fifth in ten years, one-fifth in fifteen years and one-fifth in twenty years, with interest at the rate of not less than six per centum, payable annually in advance. All sales shall be held at the county seat of the county in which the land to be sold is situate, and shall be at public auction and to the highest bidder, after sixty days' advertisement of the same in a newspaper of general circulation in the vicinity of the lands to be sold, and one at the seat of government. Such lands as shall not have been specially subdivided shall be offered in tracts of one-quarter section, and those so subdivided in the smallest subdivisions. All lands designated for sale and not sold within two years after appraisal shall be re-appraised before they are sold. No grant or patent for any such lands shall issue until payment is made for the same; provided, that the lands contracted to be sold by the state shall be subject to taxation from date of such contract. In case the taxes assessed against any of said lands for any year remain unpaid until the first Monday in October of the following year, then and thereupon the contracts of sale for such lands shall, at the election of the board of university and school lands, become null and void; and no such contract heretofore made shall be held void for non-payment of taxes accruing on the lands described therein; provided, such taxes shall have been paid before this amendment takes effect; provided, further, that any school or institution lands that may be required for townsite purposes may be paid for at any time and patent issued therefor.

results.metadata_issues: The matching session law is a PROPOSING concurrent resolution (S.B. No. 302) in the 1907 volume, not an approved/canvassed amendment. It carries NO election-canvass language and NO vote tally; its only date is "Filed in the office of the secretary of state March 23, 1907." Neither candidate file contains a vote canvass for this §158 amendment (the "Approved March 1909" lines in sl1909.txt are unrelated session-law chapters). Consequently the stored authority "Amendment ratified 1908-11-03" cannot be confirmed or backfilled from these two files — the ratification date/tally are not documented here. Stored effective_date 1908-12-03 and election_date 1908-11-03 are consistent with the proposing resolution's "next general election" referral (the 1908 general election) but are not independently corroborated by a canvass in either scan. No metadata defect detected; just note the authority field remains a placeholder lacking a session-law canvass source.

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume (E-1): /tmp/sl1907.txt, lines ~24574-24604, under "PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO CONSTITUTION" → "CONCURRENT RESOLUTION [S.B. No. 255-Little] Amending the Constitution... Relating to the Number of Judges of the Supreme Court." This is the correct source: in this era the amendment ratified at the 1908 election appears as a proposed concurrent resolution in the E-1 (1907) volume ("such amendment shall be submitted to the qualified electors of the state for approval or rejection, in accordance with... section 202"). The E+1 volume (sl1909) does NOT contain an approved canvass of this amendment; its "§ 89" (line 13282) is an unrelated MILITIA code section ("Separate companies"), so the prior pass that pointed at the wrong volume would have matched a non-constitutional section.

findings.note: Stored text matches the enacted/proposed constitutional text verbatim, word-for-word. The only divergences anywhere in the surrounding source are OCR scan artifacts in the session-law PDF in the resolution's recitals (e.g. 'tlze' for 'the', 'C oncttrring' for 'Concurring', 'het:eby' for 'hereby', 'be" amended', '-constitution'), NOT in the operative § 89 text and NOT in the STORED record. The operative section ('§ 89. The supreme court shall consist of five judges, a majority of whom shall be necessary to form a quorum or pronounce a decision, but one or more of said judges may adjourn the court from day to day or to a day certain.') renders cleanly in the scan and is identical to the stored text. Stored heading 'Number of judges of the supreme court' matches the resolution caption 'Relating to the Number of Judges of the Supreme Court.'
findings.sessionlaw_text: § 89. The supreme court shall consist of five judges, a majority of whom shall be necessary to form a quorum or pronounce a decision, but one or more of said judges may adjourn the court from day to day or to a day certain.

results.metadata_issues: Stored authority is the placeholder 'Amendment ratified 1908-11-03'. The session law (E-1, 1907) supplies a more specific, backfillable citation: 1907 N.D. Sess. Laws (Proposed Amendments to Constitution), Concurrent Resolution [S.B. No. 255-Little], 'Amending the Constitution of the State of North Dakota, Relating to the Number of Judges of the Supreme Court,' filed in the office of the secretary of state March 23, 1907. Provenance per the resolution: originally adopted by the 9th legislative assembly, referred to and agreed to by the 10th, and submitted to the qualified electors for approval/rejection at the next general election under N.D. Const. § 202. NO vote tally / canvass is present in EITHER volume — the E+1 (1909) volume contains no approved canvass of this amendment, so the election-vote count cannot be verified from these sources. Stored election_date 1908-11-03 and effective_date 1908-12-03 are consistent with the 'submit to electors at next general election' mechanism but are not independently confirmed by a canvass in either file.

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume E-1: /tmp/sl1909.txt — first §158 concurrent resolution, "SALE OF PUBLIC LANDS" [H. B. No. 146—Putnam], body at lines 18521–18554 (filed with Secretary of State March 16, 1909). This is the SHORT "purchase and sale of school and public lands" version that reduces interest to five per cent. It was adopted by the 10th legislative assembly, agreed to by the 11th (1909), and submitted to the electors at the next general election (Nov 1910) — matching this amendment's ratification. NOTE on disambiguation: the same 1909 file also holds a SECOND, LONGER §158 resolution [S.B. 114—Purcell, "Sale of Public Lands to Railroads," lines 18560–18618], and the APPROVED volume E+1 /tmp/sl1911.txt Chapter 88 [S.B. 138, lines 9148–9200] is the continuation of that railroad version (referred 11th→12th legislature, submitted Nov 1912). Both of those are a DIFFERENT, later (1912) §158 amendment and were correctly rejected.

findings.note: Stored text matches the enacted first-resolution (H.B. 146) text verbatim; difflib word ratio 0.9593 and EVERY divergence is session-law SCAN OCR noise, not a stored defect. Divergences: hyphenation line-break splits ('pur chaser'/'purchaser', 'be fore'/'before', 'expira tion'/'expiration', 'sub divided'/'subdivided', 'sub ject'/'subject', 'constitu tion'/'constitution', 'pro vided'/'provided'); spacing around punctuation ('same ; pro vided' vs 'same; provided', 'herein ; pro vided' vs 'herein; provided'); and one OCR letter substitution 'shaH' for 'shall' ('re-appraised' clause). The stored text is clean and correct. The stored text also (correctly) preserves the resolution's own slight irregularities present in the enacted text, e.g. 'and all be at public auction' and "one years' interest". Disambiguation confirmed: this is the interest-reduced-to-five-per-cent version (governor's veto message at line 19324 of the 1909 file describes the pre-amendment §158 as 'not less than six per cent', confirming the five-per-cent reduction was the amendment's purpose).
findings.sessionlaw_text: § 158. No land shall be sold for less than the appraised value, and in no case for less than ten dollars per acre. The purchaser shall pay one-fifth of the price in cash and the remaining four-fifths as follows: One-fifth in five years, one-fifth on or before the expiration of ten years, one-fifth on or before the expiration of fifteen years, and one-fifth on or before the expiration of twenty years, with interest at the rate of not less than five per cent per annum payable annually in advance; provided, that when payments are made before due they shall be made at an interest paying date, and one years' interest in advance shall be paid on all moneys so paid. All sales shall be held at the county seat of the county in which the land to be sold is situated, and all be at public auction and to the highest bidder after sixty days advertisement of the same in a newspaper in general circulation in the vicinity of the land to be sold, and also published in a newspaper published at the county seat, and also in a newspaper published at the seat of government. Such lands as shall not have been especially subdivided shall be offered in tracts of one-quarter section and those subdivided in the smallest subdivision. All lands designated for sale and not sold within two years after appraisal shall be re-appraised before they are sold. No grant or patent for such lands shall issue until payment is made for the same; provided, that the lands contracted to be sold by the state shall be subject to taxation from the date of such contract. In case the taxes assessed against any of said lands for any year remain unpaid until the first Monday in October of the following year, then and thereupon the contract of sale for such lands shall, if the board of university and school lands so determine, become null and void. Any lands under the provisions of section 158 of the constitution of the state of North Dakota that have heretofore been sold may be paid for, except as to interest, as provided herein; provided, further, that any school or institution lands that may be required for townsite purposes, may be paid for at any time and patent issued therefor.

results.metadata_issues: The matching source is a PROPOSING (E-1) volume, so it contains NO vote canvass or tally and NO approval/ratification date — concurrent resolutions here are 'Filed with the Secretary of State,' not 'Approved.' This resolution was filed March 16, 1909. No election date or vote count is available from the session law to backfill. The stored election_date 1910-11-08 and effective_date 1910-12-08 are consistent with the resolution's text ('submitted to the qualified electors of the state at the next general election' after the 1909 11th-legislature approval, i.e. Nov 1910). Stored authority 'Amendment ratified 1910-11-08' is a reasonable placeholder; provenance worth ADDING (not a defect): bill identifier 'H.B. No. 146 (Putnam)', resolution title 'A Concurrent Resolution Amending Section 158 of the Constitution ... Providing for the Purchase of and Sale of School and Public Lands,' filed March 16, 1909, citing N.D. Const. § 202 as the amendment authority. The amendment article reference is 'Article 9, section 158' (the S.B. 114 companion uses 'article 9' explicitly; the H.B. 146 enacting clause says 'Section 158' without article). No metadata in the stored record is wrong.

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume (E-1): /tmp/sl1911.txt, Chapter 88 [S.B. No. 138-Plain], "CONCURRENT RESOLUTION to Amend Section 158 of the Constitution of the State of North Dakota, Governing the Sale of School Lands to Railroad Companies" (text at lines 9148-9209, the "AMENDMENT.] Article 9, section 158 ... § 158. No land shall be sold..." block). This is the correct volume: a 1912-ratified amendment appears in the E-1 volume as a proposed concurrent resolution "submitted to the qualified electors of the state at the next general election ... in accordance with section 202." The E+1 volume (/tmp/sl1913.txt) contains NO § 158 constitutional amendment — its constitutional amendments are §§ 216, 185, etc. (Chapters 95-104), and its only "158" hits are unrelated (Chapter 158 acts, page numbers, degrees). A separate § 158-area block in the 1911 volume (lines ~19770-19900) is a statutory amendment to Revised Codes 1905 §§ 167/169/170/171/189, NOT the constitutional amendment, and was disregarded.

findings.note: Stored text matches the enacted 1911 ch. 88 concurrent resolution at a 0.918 normalized-token ratio. Every divergence is OCR garble in the session-law SCAN, with the STORED text holding the correct clean reading: e.g. scan 'r ars'/'cnt'/'inll rei t'/'dafo'/'scat'/'dnys'/'irculation'/'tbe'/'sl at'/'dcsi1matcd'/'ditehes'/'gr mnds'/'an y' vs stored 'years'/'cent'/'interest'/'date'/'seat'/'days'/'circulation'/'the'/'seat'/'designated'/'ditches'/'grounds'/'any'; plus broken-hyphenation artifacts 'sub-divided'/'sub-di vision'/'town site'/'pub lic'/'con stitution'/'prin cipal' that stored correctly closes up. No stored defect. TWO trivial, non-substantive points, both attributable to the scan and NOT stored errors: (1) scan reads 'on all money so paid' (line 9175) where stored reads 'on all moneys so paid' — OCR almost certainly dropped a faint terminal 's' ('moneys' is the conventional/parallel reading; stored is fine); (2) scan punctuates 'in full, in advance' (line 9207) vs stored 'in full in advance' — a single comma in a heavily-garbled scan, immaterial. Heading, section number (§ 158), article (Article 9), and full operative text all confirmed against the enacted resolution.
findings.sessionlaw_text: § 158. No land shall be sold for less than the appraised value, and in no case for less than ten dollars per acre. The purchaser shall pay one-fifth of the price in cash, and the remaining four-fifths as follows: One-fifth in five years, one-fifth on or before the expiration of ten years, one-fifth on or before the expiration of fifteen years, and one-fifth on or before the expiration of twenty years, with interest at the rate of not less than five per cent per annum, payable annually in advance; provided, that when payments are made before due they shall be made at an interest paying date, and one year's interest in advance shall be paid on all moneys so paid. All sales shall be held at the county seat of the county in which the land to be sold is situated, and shall be at public auction and to the highest bidder, after sixty days' advertisement of the same in a newspaper of general circulation in the vicinity of the land to be sold, and one at the seat of government. Such lands as shall not have been specially subdivided shall be offered in tracts of one-quarter section, and those subdivided in the smallest subdivisions. All lands designated for sale and not sold within two years after appraisal shall be reappraised before they are sold. No grant or patent for such lands shall issue until payment is made for the same; provided, that the land contracted to be sold by the state shall be subject to taxation from the date of contract. In case the taxes assessed against any of said lands for any year remain unpaid until the first Monday in October of the following year, then thereupon the contract of sale for such lands shall, if the board of university and school lands so determine, become null and void. Any lands under the provisions of section 158 of the constitution of the state of North Dakota that have heretofore been sold, may be paid for, except as to interest, as provided, further, that any school or institution lands that may be required for townsite purposes, school house sites, church sites, cemetery sites, sites for other educational or charitable institutions, public parks, fair grounds, public highways, railroad right of way, or for other railroad uses and purposes, reservoirs for the storage of water for irrigation, drain ditches or irrigation ditches, and lands that may be required for any of the purposes over which the right of eminent domain may be exercised under the constitution and the laws of the state of North Dakota, may be sold under the provisions of this section, and shall be paid for, principal and interest, in full, in advance, at the time of sale, or at any time thereafter, and patent issued therefor, when principal and interest are paid.

results.metadata_issues: Authority field is a placeholder ("Amendment ratified 1912-11-05") and is worth backfilling. The enacting session-law citation is: 1911 N.D. Sess. Laws ch. 88 (S.B. No. 138), "Concurrent Resolution to Amend Section 158 of the Constitution ... Governing the Sale of School Lands to Railroad Companies." The resolution recites it was adopted by the Eleventh Legislative Assembly, referred to the Twelfth for approval, and to be submitted to the qualified electors at the next general election under § 202 of the Constitution. NO vote tally / canvass / approval date appears in either candidate volume — consistent with the era's convention of printing the proposing concurrent resolution in the E-1 volume rather than an approved canvass in the E+1 volume. Stored election_date 1912-11-05 (general election) and effective_date 1912-12-05 are consistent with ratification at the 1912 general election; the session law itself does not supply an independent ratification/proclamation date to corroborate the exact dates. Section number (§ 158) and article (Article 9) in the stored record are confirmed correct against the session law.

results.source_used: PROPOSING volume (E-1): /tmp/sl1911.txt, Chapter 87 [S.B. No. 135], "CONCURRENT RESOLUTION Amending the Constitution of the State of North Dakota, Empowering the Legislative Assembly to Provide by Law for the Erection, Leasing, Purchasing, and Operating Terminal Elevators in the States of Minnesota and Wisconsin, or Either" (lines ~9114-9140). This era used the proposing-resolution convention: the amendment was adopted by the Eleventh Legislative Assembly, referred to the Twelfth, and "submitted to the qualified electors of the state at the next general election" (1912) under Section 202. The 1913 (E+1) volume contains NO Minnesota/Wisconsin amendment and NO vote canvass for it; its terminal-elevator material (Chapters re: terminal elevators in North Dakota / a Wisconsin terminal-elevator system fund) belongs to DIFFERENT amendment lines (cf. sl1911 Chapter 90, a separate North Dakota terminal-elevator amendment). The correct match for amendment 14 is Chapter 87 in the 1911 volume.

findings.note: Stored text matches the session-law (1911 Ch. 87) AMENDMENT clause word-for-word. The only differences are OCR scan artifacts in the session-law scan, NOT defects in the STORED text: the scan reads 'maintained and oper.ated' (stray period), and surrounding prose has garble like 'ancLby it', '~pproval', 'T1welfth', 'oper.ated'. Stored text is clean and correct. Section/article and subject (terminal grain elevators in Minnesota or Wisconsin) are correct.
findings.sessionlaw_text: The legislative assembly is hereby authorized and empowered to provide by law for the erection, purchasing or leasing and operation of one or more terminal grain elevators in the states of Minnesota or Wisconsin, or both, to be maintained and operated in such manner as the legislative assembly shall prescribe, and provide for inspection, weighing and grading of all grain received in such elevator or elevators.

results.metadata_issues: Stored authority is a placeholder ("Amendment ratified 1912-11-05") and should be backfilled with the proposing session law: 1911 N.D. Sess. Laws ch. 87 (S.B. No. 135), a concurrent resolution. The resolution states the amendment was "adopted by the Eleventh Legislative Assembly ... and by it referred to the Twelfth Legislative Assembly ... for approval or rejection," then "submitted to the qualified electors of the state at the next general election for approval or rejection in accordance with the provisions of Section 202 of the Constitution." This is the PROPOSING (E-1) volume, so it contains NO vote tally / canvass. No approval/election date or vote-count canvass for this Minnesota/Wisconsin amendment appears in either the 1911 or the 1913 volume (the 1913 volume has no Minnesota/Wisconsin terminal-elevator amendment at all). The stored election_date 1912-11-05 and effective_date 1912-12-05 cannot be confirmed against a canvass in these two files; they are not contradicted, but the vote tally that would normally appear in the E+1 approved volume is absent here. CAUTION on prior-pass error: sl1911 Chapter 90 (S.B. No. 229) is a SEPARATE amendment for terminal elevators in the State of NORTH DAKOTA, and the 1913 volume's terminal-elevator entries are likewise different lines — do not match those to amendment 14.

results.source_used: /tmp/sl1919.txt (E+1 APPROVED volume, 1919 Session Laws), CHAPTER 87 (H.B. No. 11, Committee State Affairs) — Concurrent Resolution agreeing to a proposed amendment to Section 177, Article 11, "Relative to the Assessment of Improvements on Land and Authorizing a Tax on Lands for the Purpose of Indemnifying Owners of Growing Crops Against Damages by Hail," proposed by initiative petition, which received a majority at the general election held November 5, 1918, "Approved January 20, 1919." The 1917 volume's Section 177 amendment (sl1917 CHAPTER 92, approved Feb 14, 1917) is a DIFFERENT amendment about "Taxes of Improvements on Land" ("All improvements on land shall be assessed..."); the prior pass likely matched that wrong one.

findings.sessionlaw_text: The legislature may by law provide for the levy and collection of an acreage tax on lands within the state in addition to the limitations specified in Section 174 in Article 11 of the Constitution. The proceeds of such tax shall be used to indemnify the owners of growing crops against damages by hail, provided that lands used exclusively for public roads, rights of way of common carriers, mining, manufacturing or pasturage may be exempt from such tax.
findings.note: Single-word divergence in the STORED text. The session law (sl1919 CHAPTER 87, line 5931) reads plural "limitations" (OCR "li111itatio11s s1w1.:ilk1l" = "limitations specified"; the trailing -s is a distinct token-final character before the space preceding "specified"), whereas the STORED text reads singular "limitation" ("in addition to the limitation specified in Section 174"). Every other word matches the session law: subject matter (acreage tax to indemnify owners of growing crops against hail), the Section 174/Article 11 cross-reference, and the exclusively-public-roads/rights-of-way/common-carriers/mining/manufacturing/pasturage exemption clause. Confidence on the limitations/limitation difference is MODERATE because the scan is heavily garbled; the rest of the text is confidently identical (OCR-only garble). What would raise confidence: a clean compiled-constitution or Blue Book copy of the 1918 §177 hail amendment to confirm plural "limitations."

results.metadata_issues: Session law (sl1919 CHAPTER 87) confirms: proposed by initiative petition by the people; "received a majority of all the legal votes cast at the general election held in this state on November 5, 1918"; referred to the Sixteenth Legislative Assembly; declared a part of the Constitution; "Approved January 20, 1919." STORED election_date 1918-11-05 matches the canvassed election date. STORED effective_date is 1918-12-05 — the session law gives the legislative approval/declaration date as January 20, 1919, not December 5, 1918; the 12-05 effective date is not supported by this session-law text and may warrant review (the operative declaration date in the session law is 1919-01-20). STORED authority is a placeholder ("Amendment ratified 1918-11-05") that could be backfilled to: "1919 N.D. Sess. Laws ch. 87 (H.B. No. 11); initiative petition, ratified at the general election Nov. 5, 1918, declared part of the Constitution Jan. 20, 1919." No numeric vote tally is printed (the resolution states only "a majority of all the legal votes cast").

results.source_used: Primary authoritative match: /tmp/sl1921.txt (E+1 APPROVED volume), Session Laws 1921, the canvass/compilation of adopted constitutional amendments, "ARTICLE 37" (lines ~13833-13846): "Section 121. ... Adopted November 2, 1920. 129,628 to 63,569. Chapter 86, Session Laws 1917; Chapter 94, Session Laws 1919." Cross-confirmed against the E-1 PROPOSING volume /tmp/sl1919.txt, Chapter 92 (S.B. No. 81-McCarten), "Concurrent Resolution ... Providing for the Elective Franchises," § 121 amendment, "Approved February 18, 1919" (lines ~6197-6228). The 1921 ARTICLE 37 is the correct match because it is dated "Adopted November 2, 1920" with a vote canvass — exactly THIS amendment. I deliberately rejected /tmp/sl1921.txt Chapter 41 (S.B. No. 57-Wenstrom, line ~4815), a DIFFERENT, later § 121 residence-requirement amendment "Approved March 2, 1921" submitted for the June 1922 primary; and ARTICLE 36 (a separate residence amendment).

findings.note: Body text is word-for-word IDENTICAL to the authoritative 1921 approved canvass (ARTICLE 37) and to the 1919 proposing enactment (Chapter 92). Only two non-substantive style points, both attributable to source/scan rather than a STORED defect: (1) Section label — STORED reads 'Sec. 121.', matching the 1919 proposing volume's 'Sec. 12i.' (OCR of 'Sec. 121.', the final 1 mis-read as i); the 1921 canvass spells it 'Section 121.'. The two session-law volumes themselves disagree on the abbreviation, and STORED follows the enacting resolution's 'Sec.' form. (2) Enumerator case — STORED 'Second,' (capitalized) matches the 1919 proposing volume's 'Second,'; the 1921 canvass prints lowercase 'second,'. Again a volume-vs-volume style divergence, not a STORED error. The 1919 OCR also shows scan noise ('First citizens' without comma, 'India~ descent', 'tbe-ir tribal relation') that the clean 1921 canvass renders correctly as 'First, citizens', 'Indian descent', 'their tribal relation' — STORED already has the correct clean forms. STORED text is fine; defects noted are SCAN/style only.
findings.sessionlaw_text: Section 121. Every person of the age of twenty-one years or upwards, belonging to either of the following classes who shall have resided in the state one year and in the county ninety days and in the precinct thirty days next preceding any election shall be a qualified elector at such election. First, citizens of the United States; second, civilized persons of Indian descent who have severed their tribal relation two years next preceding such election.

results.metadata_issues: From the authoritative 1921 approved canvass (ARTICLE 37, /tmp/sl1921.txt lines ~13833-13846): Adopted November 2, 1920 (matches STORED election_date 1920-11-02); vote tally 129,628 to 63,569 (for to against); statutory authority chain "Chapter 86, Session Laws 1917; Chapter 94, Session Laws 1919." The E-1 proposing resolution in /tmp/sl1919.txt is Chapter 92 (S.B. No. 81-McCarten), approved February 18, 1919 — note the 1921 canvass attributes ARTICLE 37 to Ch. 94/1919 rather than Ch. 92, a discrepancy worth a human check before backfill (the § 121 woman-suffrage resolution I located in 1919 is labeled Chapter 92). STORED authority field is the placeholder "Amendment ratified 1920-11-02"; recommend backfilling with the session-law chapter citations and the 129,628-to-63,569 canvass result. STORED effective_date 1920-12-02 (post-election certification) is consistent with a Nov. 2, 1920 adoption. No date/section/article metadata in STORED is wrong; the only actionable item is enriching the placeholder authority.
